brokers.name: Acxiom (now IPG subsidiary)
brokers.description: Maintains marketing databases on virtually every US consumer, selling demographic, behavioral, and purchase data to advertisers and enterprises.
brokers.revenue: $1.1B revenue (acquired by IPG for $2.3B in 2018; LiveRamp spun off separately)
brokers.controversy: Maintained files on 700+ million consumers worldwide with up to 1,500 data points each; a 2003 insider breach exposed data on millions.

brokers.name: LiveRamp
brokers.description: Operates identity resolution platform connecting offline consumer data to digital advertising IDs, enabling cross-device targeting at massive scale.
brokers.controversy: Capitol Forum investigation (2025) revealed LiveRamp marketplace hosted datasets targeting Americans by sensitive attributes including military status and health conditions.

brokers.name: Experian
brokers.description: Collects and sells credit data, marketing lists, and consumer profiles to financial institutions, insurers, landlords, and marketers worldwide.
brokers.controversy: 2015 breach exposed 15 million T-Mobile customer records; repeatedly fined for selling consumer data without adequate consent across multiple jurisdictions.

brokers.name: TransUnion
brokers.description: Sells credit reports, fraud detection, and marketing audience data derived from credit histories and public records on 1 billion consumers globally.
brokers.controversy: FTC fined TransUnion $312M in 2024 for deceptive marketing of credit monitoring products; also sold pseudonymized military personnel data via LiveRamp marketplace.

brokers.name: Equifax
brokers.description: Aggregates credit, employment, and income data on 800+ million consumers and sells risk scoring, identity verification, and marketing data.
brokers.controversy: 2017 breach exposed personal data of 147 million Americans including SSNs; settled for $700M — one of the largest data breaches in history.

brokers.name: LexisNexis Risk Solutions (RELX)
brokers.description: Sells comprehensive consumer risk profiles, identity data, and public records to insurers, law enforcement, and financial institutions.
brokers.controversy: Sold data to ICE enabling immigration enforcement; 2025 breach confirmed exposure of internal and customer data; privacy advocates call it a 'shadow credit bureau.'

brokers.name: Epsilon (Publicis Groupe)
brokers.description: Maintains one of the world's largest consumer marketing databases with profiles on 250+ million Americans, powering targeted advertising campaigns.
brokers.revenue: $4.4B acquisition price (Publicis, 2019); contributes ~$2.5B annually to Publicis revenue
brokers.controversy: 2011 email breach exposed names and email addresses from databases of 75+ major brands including Citibank, JPMorgan Chase, and Best Buy.

brokers.name: Oracle Data Cloud (Oracle Advertising)
brokers.description: Aggregated third-party audience data from 200+ providers for ad targeting; Oracle shut down its advertising business in 2024 amid privacy headwinds.
brokers.controversy: Oracle shut down its entire advertising division in June 2024 citing regulatory risk; had previously faced GDPR complaints and a class-action lawsuit for mass surveillance of internet users.

brokers.name: CoreLogic
brokers.description: Collects and sells property, mortgage, rental history, and homeowner data used by insurers, lenders, and real estate firms for risk assessment.
brokers.revenue: $1.6B revenue (before 2021 private acquisition at $6B by Stone Point/Insight Partners)
brokers.controversy: Congress investigated CoreLogic in 2023 for selling sensitive property and mortgage data; landlords use CoreLogic tenant screening to deny housing to millions.

brokers.name: Verisk Analytics
brokers.description: Sells insurance claims data, property risk scores, and consumer risk profiles to insurance companies, enabling premium pricing and fraud detection.
brokers.controversy: Operates ISO ClaimSearch with 1.4 billion insurance claims records; critics argue it creates an opaque scoring system that raises premiums without consumer knowledge or consent.

brokers.name: Nielsen
brokers.description: Measures TV viewership and consumer purchasing behavior, selling audience data and shopper insights to media companies and advertisers.
brokers.controversy: MRC suspended Nielsen's accreditation in 2021 for undercounting TV audiences during the pandemic, shaking industry trust in the measurement monopoly.

brokers.name: Lotame
brokers.description: Operates a data management platform and data exchange, selling second and third-party audience segments to advertisers for cross-device targeting.
brokers.controversy: Privacy advocates have criticized Lotame's data marketplace for enabling the sale of sensitive behavioral segments with minimal transparency to consumers.

brokers.name: The Trade Desk
brokers.description: Operates the largest independent demand-side platform, enabling advertisers to buy targeted ads using vast consumer data from 100+ data partners.
knownBuyers: Advertisers buy through TTD; they are the buy-side platform
brokers.controversy: Unified ID 2.0 initiative criticized by privacy advocates as a cookie replacement that still enables pervasive tracking; stock dropped 30%+ after Q4 2024 earnings miss.

brokers.name: Google (DV360 / Google Ads)
brokers.description: Collects data from 4.3 billion users across Search, YouTube, Gmail, Maps, Android, and Chrome to power the world's largest targeted advertising system.
knownBuyers: Millions of advertisers worldwide via self-serve and DV360
brokers.controversy: DOJ antitrust case (2024-2025) found Google maintained illegal monopoly in ad tech; EU fined Google €4.34B for Android antitrust violations; settled $5B Incognito mode tracking lawsuit.

brokers.name: Meta (Facebook/Instagram)
brokers.description: Harvests behavioral, social, and demographic data from 3.9 billion monthly users to sell hyper-targeted advertising across Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp.
sources: Meta Pixel on millions of websites
brokers.controversy: Cambridge Analytica scandal exposed that data on 87 million users was harvested without consent for political manipulation; $5B FTC fine in 2019.

brokers.name: Amazon (Amazon DSP / AWS Data Exchange)
brokers.description: Leverages purchase history, browsing, Alexa voice data, and Ring footage to power targeted advertising and sells data via AWS Data Exchange marketplace.
knownBuyers: Third parties via AWS Data Exchange
brokers.controversy: FTC fined Amazon $25M in 2023 for retaining children's Alexa voice recordings; Ring shared video with police without warrants; workers listened to Alexa recordings.

brokers.name: Criteo
brokers.description: Tracks users across the web via cookies and retailer partnerships to serve retargeted ads, maintaining profiles on 2.5 billion monthly users.
brokers.controversy: CNIL (France) fined Criteo €40M in 2023 for GDPR violations — tracking users without proper consent and failing to honor opt-out requests.

brokers.name: Taboola
brokers.description: Places 'recommended content' widgets on major news sites, tracking user clicks and browsing behavior to serve targeted native advertising.
brokers.controversy: Widely criticized for promoting misleading clickbait, scam ads, and low-quality content alongside legitimate journalism on premium publisher sites.

brokers.name: Outbrain
brokers.description: Operates a content recommendation platform on major publishers, tracking user behavior to serve personalized native ads and content suggestions.
brokers.controversy: Same clickbait criticism as Taboola; acquired Teads for $1B in 2024 to pivot toward programmatic video after years of stagnant growth.

brokers.name: Segment (Twilio)
brokers.description: Customer data platform embedded in 25,000+ websites/apps that collects, unifies, and routes user behavioral data to hundreds of downstream tools.
brokers.revenue: Part of Twilio ($4B total revenue); Segment acquired for $3.2B in 2020
brokers.controversy: Acts as a data pipeline enabling companies to send user data to dozens of third parties simultaneously, often without clear user awareness of all downstream recipients.

brokers.name: Amplitude
brokers.description: Provides product analytics tracking every user action in apps and websites, building detailed behavioral profiles used for product decisions and targeting.
knownBuyers: Data flows to client's own tools; integrates with ad platforms, CDPs
brokers.controversy: SDK tracks granular user behavior (every tap, scroll, click) creating detailed behavioral profiles; critics question whether end users truly consent to this level of tracking.

brokers.name: Mixpanel
brokers.description: Event-based analytics platform tracking user actions across apps and websites, enabling companies to build behavioral profiles for product and marketing decisions.
knownBuyers: Internal use by clients; data routed to marketing and ad platforms
brokers.controversy: Accidentally collected user IP addresses when claiming not to; faced criticism for making it easy for companies to track individual users without explicit consent.

brokers.name: Palantir Technologies
brokers.description: Builds data integration and surveillance platforms for government agencies, fusing consumer data, financial records, and communications into actionable intelligence.
brokers.controversy: Built ICE deportation tracking system; 2025-2026 expansion into IRS and Social Security drew Sen. Wyden investigation; critics call it a 'privatized surveillance state.'

brokers.name: Clearview AI
brokers.description: Scraped 40+ billion facial images from the internet without consent to build a facial recognition database sold to law enforcement worldwide.
brokers.controversy: Fined $33M by Dutch DPA (2024), €20M by Italy, £7.5M by UK; built database by mass-scraping social media in violation of platform ToS; called 'the end of privacy as we know it.'

brokers.name: Babel Street
brokers.description: Sells location tracking and social media monitoring tools to US intelligence agencies, military, and law enforcement using commercially available data.
brokers.controversy: Locate X tool let government agencies track Americans' movements via commercial data without warrants, circumventing Fourth Amendment protections.

brokers.name: Venntel (now part of Gravy Analytics)
brokers.description: Sold precise smartphone location data derived from advertising bid streams to federal agencies for warrantless surveillance of Americans' movements.
brokers.controversy: FTC took action in December 2024 for unlawfully selling location data that tracked people to sensitive sites including reproductive health clinics, domestic violence shelters, and places of worship.

brokers.name: Fog Data Science
brokers.description: Sold mass smartphone location surveillance to local police departments for as little as $7,500/year, enabling warrantless tracking of entire populations.
brokers.controversy: EFF/AP investigation (2022) revealed Fog sold location tracking to ~40 local police departments, enabling surveillance of people visiting abortion clinics, mosques, and protests.

brokers.name: ZoomInfo
brokers.description: Scrapes the web, email signatures, and business communications to build and sell detailed B2B contact databases with 260M+ professional profiles.
brokers.controversy: Accused of scraping personal data without consent; stock crashed 70%+ in 2023-2024 as customers questioned data quality; aggressive sales tactics widely criticized.

brokers.name: Clearbit (now HubSpot)
brokers.description: Enriches business contacts by scraping web data and matching email addresses to build profiles with company, role, and technographic data.
brokers.controversy: Built profiles on millions of professionals without their knowledge or consent by scraping publicly available data and matching it to email addresses.

brokers.name: FICO
brokers.description: Creates credit scores used in 90% of US lending decisions and sells predictive analytics models that determine consumers' access to credit, insurance, and housing.
brokers.controversy: CFPB investigated FICO and credit bureaus for opaque scoring that perpetuates racial disparities in lending; FICO score changes can cost consumers thousands in higher interest rates.

brokers.name: Gravy Analytics
brokers.description: Collects precise location data from millions of smartphones via advertising bid streams and sells movement patterns and foot traffic analytics.
brokers.controversy: FTC action in December 2024 for selling location data tracking consumers to sensitive locations; January 2025 data breach exposed precise location data of millions.

brokers.name: Kochava
brokers.description: Mobile attribution and analytics platform that collected and sold precise location data enabling tracking of individuals to sensitive locations.
brokers.controversy: FTC sued Kochava in 2022 for selling data that could track people to reproductive health clinics, addiction treatment centers, and domestic violence shelters; settled in 2024.

brokers.name: X-Mode Social (now Outlogic)
brokers.description: Embedded location-tracking SDKs in hundreds of apps including Muslim prayer apps, then sold precise location data to military contractors and data brokers.
brokers.controversy: Vice/Motherboard revealed X-Mode sold location data from Muslim prayer apps to US military contractors; FTC forced consent decree in January 2024.

brokers.name: Mobilewalla
brokers.description: Collects mobile device location and behavioral data from billions of devices, selling consumer profiles and audience segments to marketers and governments.
brokers.controversy: Used location data to track and profile Black Lives Matter protesters in 2020; FTC took action in December 2024 alongside Gravy Analytics and Venntel.

brokers.name: Snowflake
brokers.description: Cloud data platform with a marketplace where companies buy and sell consumer datasets, acting as infrastructure for the entire data broker ecosystem.
brokers.controversy: Massive 2024 breach via stolen credentials exposed data from 165+ customers including AT&T (110M records), Ticketmaster (560M), Santander Bank, and Advance Auto Parts.

brokers.name: Anomaly Six (A6)
brokers.description: Embedded tracking SDKs in hundreds of popular apps to collect location data, then sold surveillance capabilities to US defense and intelligence agencies.
brokers.controversy: Wall Street Journal revealed A6 could track billions of phones globally including monitoring CIA and NSA employees' movements via their own personal devices.

stats.averageAmericansProfiles: 1,500+ data points per person across major brokers
stats.dataPointsPerPerson: Acxiom alone maintains up to 1,500 data points on each of 700M+ consumers globally
stats.ftcActions2024: FTC took action against Gravy Analytics, Venntel, Kochava, X-Mode, and InMarket in 2024 alone for location data abuses
stats.note: Based on FTC 2024 enforcement actions, Mordor Intelligence 2026 market report, and public company filings. Market size estimates vary ($131B-$315B for 2024-2026) depending on scope of 'data broker' definition.
stats.keyTrend: Oracle shut down its entire advertising data business in 2024; regulatory pressure accelerating with FTC, EU GDPR, and state privacy laws
sources: Mordor Intelligence Data Broker Market Report (2026)
Wall Street Journal Anomaly Six Investigation
